return {l for l in labels if re.match(label_regex, l)}\n\n\ndef filter(test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]], labels: set[str]) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Select the list of test config to run from the test matrix. The logic works\n    as follows:\n\n    If the PR has one or more test-config labels as specified, only these test configs\n    will be selected.  This also works with ciflow labels, for example, if a PR has both\n    ciflow/trunk and test-config/functorch, only trunk functorch builds and tests will\n    be run.\n\n    If the PR has none of the test-config label, all tests are run as usual.\n    \"\"\"\n    filtered_test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]] = {\"include\": []}\n\n    for entry in test_matrix.get(\"include\", []):\n        config_name = entry.get(\"config\", \"\")\n        if not config_name:\n            continue\n\n        label = f\"{PREFIX}{config_name.strip()}\"\n        if label in labels:\n            msg = f\"Select {config_name} because label {label} is present in the pull request by the time the test starts\"\n            info(msg)\n            filtered_test_matrix[\"include\"].append(entry)\n\n    test_config_labels = filter_labels(labels, re.compile(f\"{PREFIX}.+\"))\n    if not filtered_test_matrix[\"include\"] and not test_config_labels:\n        info(\"Found no test-config label on the PR, so all test configs are included\")\n        # Found no test-config label and the filtered test matrix is empty, return the same\n        # test matrix as before so that all tests can be run normally\n        return test_matrix\n    else:\n        msg = f\"Found {test_config_labels} on the PR so only these test configs are run\"\n        info(msg)\n        # When the filter test matrix contain matches or if a valid test config label\n        # is found in the PR, return the filtered test matrix\n        return filtered_test_matrix\n\n\ndef filter_selected_test_configs(\n    test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]], selected_test_configs: set[str]\n) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Keep only the selected configs if the list if not empty. Otherwise, keep all test configs.\n    This filter is used when the workflow is dispatched manually.\n    \"\"\"\n    if not selected_test_configs:\n        return test_matrix\n\n    filtered_test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]] = {\"include\": []}\n    for entry in test_matrix.get(\"include\", []):\n        config_name = entry.get(\"config\", \"\")\n        if not config_name:\n            continue\n\n        if config_name in selected_test_configs:\n            filtered_test_matrix[\"include\"].append(entry)\n\n    return filtered_test_matrix\n\n\ndef set_periodic_modes(\n    test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]], job_name: Optional[str]\n) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Apply all periodic modes when running under a schedule\n    \"\"\"\n    scheduled_test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]] = {\n        \"include\": [],\n    }\n\n    for config in test_matrix.get(\"include\", []):\n        for mode, cond in SUPPORTED_PERIODICAL_MODES.items():\n            if not cond(job_name):\n                continue\n\n            cfg = config.copy()\n            cfg[mode] = mode\n            scheduled_test_matrix[\"include\"].append(cfg)\n\n    return scheduled_test_matrix\n\n\ndef mark_unstable_jobs(\n    workflow: str, job_name: str, test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]]\n) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Check the list of unstable jobs and mark them accordingly. Note that if a job\n    is unstable, all its dependents will also be marked accordingly\n    \"\"\"\n    return process_jobs(\n        workflow=workflow,\n        job_name=job_name,\n        test_matrix=test_matrix,\n        issue_type=IssueType.UNSTABLE,\n        url=UNSTABLE_JOBS_URL,\n    )\n\n\ndef remove_disabled_jobs(\n    workflow: str, job_name: str, test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]]\n) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Check the list of disabled jobs, remove the current job and all its dependents\n    if it exists in the list\n    \"\"\"\n    return process_jobs(\n        workflow=workflow,\n        job_name=job_name,\n        test_matrix=test_matrix,\n        issue_type=IssueType.DISABLED,\n        url=DISABLED_JOBS_URL,\n    )\n\n\ndef _filter_jobs(\n    test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]],\n    issue_type: IssueType,\n    target_cfg: Optional[str] = None,\n) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    An utility function used to actually apply the job filter\n    \"\"\"\n    # The result will be stored here\n    filtered_test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]] = {\"include\": []}\n\n    # This is an issue to disable a CI job\n    if issue_type == IssueType.DISABLED:\n        # If there is a target config, disable (remove) only that\n        if target_cfg:\n            # Remove the target config from the test matrix\n            filtered_test_matrix[\"include\"] = [\n                r for r in test_matrix[\"include\"] if r.get(\"config\", \"\") != target_cfg\n            ]\n\n        return filtered_test_matrix\n\n    if issue_type == IssueType.UNSTABLE:\n        for r in test_matrix[\"include\"]:\n            cpy = r.copy()\n\n            if (target_cfg and r.get(\"config\", \"\") == target_cfg) or not target_cfg:\n                # If there is a target config, only mark that as unstable, otherwise,\n                # mark everything as unstable\n                cpy[IssueType.UNSTABLE.value] = IssueType.UNSTABLE.value\n\n            filtered_test_matrix[\"include\"].append(cpy)\n\n        return filtered_test_matrix\n\n    # No matching issue, return everything\n    return test_matrix\n\n\ndef process_jobs(\n    workflow: str,\n    job_name: str,\n    test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]],\n    issue_type: IssueType,\n    url: str,\n) -> dict[str, list[Any]]:\n    \"\"\"\n    Both disabled and unstable jobs are in the following format:\n\n    {\n        \"WORKFLOW / PLATFORM / JOB (CONFIG)\": [\n            AUTHOR,\n            ISSUE_NUMBER,\n            ISSUE_URL,\n            WORKFLOW,\n            PLATFORM,\n            JOB (CONFIG),\n        ],\n        \"pull / linux-bionic-py3.8-clang9 / test (dynamo)\": [\n            \"pytorchbot\",\n            \"94861\",\n            \"https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/94861\",\n            \"pull\",\n            \"linux-bionic-py3.8-clang9\",\n            \"test (dynamo)\",\n        ],\n    }\n    \"\"\"\n    try:\n        # The job name from github is in the PLATFORM / JOB (CONFIG) format, so breaking\n        # it into its two components first\n        current_platform, _ = (n.strip() for n in job_name.split(JOB_NAME_SEP, 1) if n)\n    except ValueError:\n        warnings.warn(f\"Invalid job name {job_name}, returning\")\n        return test_matrix\n\n    for record in download_json(url=url, headers={}).values():\n        (\n            author,\n            _,\n            target_url,\n            target_workflow,\n            target_platform,\n            target_job_cfg,\n        ) = record\n\n        if target_workflow != workflow:\n            # The current workflow doesn't match this record\n            continue\n\n        cleanup_regex = rf\"(-{BUILD_JOB_NAME}|-{TEST_JOB_NAME})$\"\n        # There is an exception here for binary build workflows in which the platform\n        # names have the build and test suffix. For example, we have a build job called\n        # manywheel-py3-cuda11_8-build / build and its subsequent test job called\n        # manywheel-py3-cuda11_8-test / test. So they are linked, but their suffixes\n        # are different\n        target_platform_no_suffix = re.sub(cleanup_regex, \"\", target_platform)\n        current_platform_no_suffix = re.sub(cleanup_regex, \"\", current_platform)\n\n        if (\n            target_platform != current_platform\n            and target_platform_no_suffix != current_platform_no_suffix\n        ):\n            # The current platform doesn't match this record\n            continue\n\n        # The logic after this is fairly complicated:\n        #\n        # - If the target record doesn't have the optional job (config) name,\n        #   i.e. pull / linux-bionic-py3.8-clang9, all build and test jobs will\n        #   be skipped if it's a disabled record or marked as unstable if it's\n        #   an unstable record\n        #\n        # - If the target record has the job name and it's a build job, i.e.\n        #   pull / linux-bionic-py3.8-clang9 / build, all build and test jobs\n        #   will be skipped if it's a disabled record or marked as unstable if\n        #   it's an unstable record, because the latter requires the former\n        #\n        # - If the target record has the job name and it's a test job without\n        #   the config part, i.e. pull / linux-bionic-py3.8-clang9 / test, all\n        #   test jobs will be skipped if it's a disabled record or marked as\n        #   unstable if it's an unstable record\n        #\n        # - If the target record has the job (config) name, only that test config\n        #   will be skipped or marked as unstable\n        if not target_job_cfg:\n            msg = (\n                f\"Issue {target_url} created by {author} has {issue_type.value} \"\n                + f\"all CI jobs for {workflow} / {job_name}\"\n            )\n            info(msg)\n            return _filter_jobs(\n                test_matrix=test_matrix,\n                issue_type=issue_type,\n            )\n\n        if target_job_cfg == BUILD_JOB_NAME:\n            msg = (\n                f\"Issue {target_url} created by {author} has {issue_type.value} \"\n                + f\"the build job for {workflow} / {job_name}\"\n            )\n            info(msg)\n            return _filter_jobs(\n                test_matrix=test_matrix,\n                issue_type=issue_type,\n            )\n\n        if target_job_cfg in (TEST_JOB_NAME, BUILD_AND_TEST_JOB_NAME):\n            msg = (\n                f\"Issue {target_url} created by {author} has {issue_type.value} \"\n                + f\"all the test jobs for {workflow} / {job_name}\"\n            )\n            info(msg)\n            return _filter_jobs(\n                test_matrix=test_matrix,\n                issue_type=issue_type,\n            )\n\n        m = JOB_NAME_CFG_REGEX.match(target_job_cfg)\n        if m:\n            target_job = m.group(\"job\")\n            # Make sure that the job name is a valid test job name first before checking the config\n            if target_job in (TEST_JOB_NAME, BUILD_AND_TEST_JOB_NAME):\n                target_cfg = m.group(\"cfg\")\n\n                # NB: There can be multiple unstable configurations, i.e. inductor, inductor_huggingface\n                test_matrix = _filter_jobs(\n                    test_matrix=test_matrix,\n                    issue_type=issue_type,\n                    target_cfg=target_cfg,\n                )\n        else:\n            warnings.warn(\n                f\"Found a matching {issue_type.value} issue {target_url} for {workflow} / {job_name}, \"\n                + f\"but the name {target_job_cfg} is invalid\"\n            )\n\n    # Found no matching target, return the same input test matrix\n    return test_matrix\n\n\ndef download_json(url: str, headers: dict[str, str], num_retries: int = 3) -> Any:\n    for _ in range(num_retries):\n        try:\n            req = Request(url=url, headers=headers)\n            content = urlopen(req, timeout=5).read().decode(\"utf-8\")\n            return json.loads(content)\n        except Exception as e:\n            warnings.warn(f\"Could not download {url}: {e}\")\n\n    warnings.warn(f\"All {num_retries} retries exhausted, downloading {url} failed\")\n    return {}\n\n\ndef set_output(name: str, val: Any) -> None:\n    print(f\"Setting output {name}={val}\")\n    if os.getenv(\"GITHUB_OUTPUT\"):\n        with open(str(os.getenv(\"GITHUB_OUTPUT\")), \"a\") as env:\n            print(f\"{name}={val}\", file=env)\n    else:\n        print(f\"::set-output name={name}::{val}\")\n\n\ndef parse_reenabled_issues(s: Optional[str]) -> list[str]:\n    # NB: When the PR body is empty, GitHub API returns a None value, which is\n    # passed into this function\n    if not s:\n        return []\n\n    # The regex is meant to match all *case-insensitive* keywords that\n    # GitHub has delineated would link PRs to issues, more details here:\n    # https://docs.github.com/en/issues/tracking-your-work-with-issues/linking-a-pull-request-to-an-issue.\n    # E.g., \"Close #62851\", \"fixES #62851\" and \"RESOLVED #62851\" would all match, but not\n    # \"closes  #62851\" --> extra space, \"fixing #62851\" --> not a keyword, nor \"fix 62851\" --> no #\n    issue_numbers = [x[5] for x in re.findall(REENABLE_TEST_REGEX, s)]\n    return issue_numbers\n\n\ndef get_reenabled_issues(pr_body: str = \"\") -> list[str]:\n    default_branch = f\"origin/{os.environ.get('GIT_DEFAULT_BRANCH', 'main')}\"\n    try:\n        commit_messages = subprocess.check_output(\n            f\"git cherry -v {default_branch}\".split(\" \")\n        ).decode(\"utf-8\")\n    except Exception as e:\n        warnings.warn(f\"failed to get commit messages: {e}\")\n        commit_messages = \"\"\n    return parse_reenabled_issues(pr_body) + parse_reenabled_issues(commit_messages)\n\n\ndef check_for_setting(labels: set[str], body: str, setting: str) -> bool:\n    return setting in labels or f\"[{setting}]\" in body\n\n\ndef perform_misc_tasks(\n    labels: set[str],\n    test_matrix: dict[str, list[Any]],\n    job_name: str,\n    pr_body: str,\n    branch: Optional[str] = None,\n    tag: Optional[str] = None,\n) -> None:\n    \"\"\"\n    In addition to apply the filter logic, the script also does the following\n    misc tasks to set keep-going and is-unstable variables\n    \"\"\"\n    set_output(\n        \"keep-going\",\n        branch == MAIN_BRANCH\n        or bool(tag and re.match(r\"^trunk/[a-f0-9]{40}$\", tag))\n        # Pattern for tags created via manual run on HUD\n        or bool(tag and re.match(r\"^ciflow/[^/]+/[a-f0-9]{40}$\", tag))\n        or check_for_setting(labels, pr_body, \"keep-going\"),\n    )\n    set_output(\n        \"ci-verbose-test-logs\",\n        check_for_setting(labels, pr_body, \"ci-verbose-test-logs\"),\n    )\n    set_output(\n        \"ci-test-showlocals\", check_for_setting(labels, pr_body, \"ci-test-showlocals\")\n    )\n    set_output(\n        \"ci-no-test-timeout\", check_for_setting(labels, pr_body, \"ci-no-test-timeout\")\n    )\n    set_output(\"ci-no-td\", check_for_setting(labels, pr_body, \"ci-no-td\"))\n    # Only relevant for the one linux distributed cuda job, delete this when TD\n    # is rolled out completely\n    set_output(\n        \"ci-td-distributed\", check_for_setting(labels, pr_body, \"ci-td-distributed\")\n    )\n\n    # Obviously, if the job name includes unstable, then this is an unstable job\n    is_unstable = job_name and IssueType.UNSTABLE.value in job_name\n    if not is_unstable and test_matrix and test_matrix.get(\"include\"):\n        # Even when the job name doesn't mention unstable, we will also mark it as\n        # unstable when the test matrix only includes unstable jobs. Basically, this\n        # logic allows build or build-and-test jobs to be marked as unstable too.\n        #\n        # Basically, when a build job is unstable, all the subsequent test jobs are\n        # also unstable. And when all test jobs are unstable, we will also treat the\n        # build job as unstable. It's simpler this way\n        is_unstable = all(IssueType.UNSTABLE.value in r for r in test_matrix[\"include\"])\n\n    set_output(\n        \"is-unstable\",\n        is_unstable,\n    )\n\n    set_output(\"reenabled-issues\", \",\".join(get_reenabled_issues(pr_body=pr_body)))\n\n    if MEM_LEAK_LABEL in labels:\n        # Enable mem leak check if label is added\n        for config in test_matrix.get(\"include\", []):\n            if is_cuda_or_rocm_job(job_name):\n                config[\"mem_leak_check\"] = \"mem_leak_check\"\n\n\ndef main() -> None:\n    args = parse_args()\n    # Load the original test matrix set by the workflow. Its format, however,\n    # doesn't follow the strict JSON format, so we load it using yaml here for\n    # its more relaxed syntax\n    test_matrix = yaml.safe_load(args.test_matrix)\n\n    if test_matrix is None:\n        warnings.warn(f\"Invalid test matrix input '{args.test_matrix}', exiting\")\n        # We handle invalid test matrix gracefully by marking it as empty\n        set_output(\"is-test-matrix-empty\", True)\n        sys.exit(0)\n\n    pr_number = args.pr_number\n    tag = args.tag\n\n    # If the tag matches, we can get the PR number from it, this is from ciflow\n    # workflow dispatcher\n    tag_regex = re.compile(r\"^ciflow/[\\w\\-]+/(?P<pr_number>\\d+)$\")\n\n    labels = set()\n    if pr_number:\n        # If a PR number is set, query all the labels from that PR\n        labels = get_labels(int(pr_number))\n        # Then filter the test matrix and keep only the selected ones\n        filtered_test_matrix = filter(test_matrix, labels)\n\n    elif tag:\n        m = tag_regex.match(tag)\n\n        if m:\n            pr_number = m.group(\"pr_number\")\n\n            # The PR number can also come from the tag in ciflow tag event\n            labels = get_labels(int(pr_number))\n            # Filter the test matrix and keep only the selected ones\n            filtered_test_matrix = filter(test_matrix, labels)\n\n        else:\n            # There is a tag but it isn't ciflow, so there is nothing left to do\n            filtered_test_matrix = test_matrix\n\n    else:\n        # No PR number, no tag, we can just return the test matrix as it is\n        filtered_test_matrix = test_matrix\n\n    if args.selected_test_configs:\n        selected_test_configs = {\n            v.strip().lower()\n            for v in args.selected_test_configs.split(\",\")\n            if v.strip()\n        }\n        filtered_test_matrix = filter_selected_test_configs(\n            filtered_test_matrix, selected_test_configs\n        )\n\n    if args.event_name == \"schedule\" and args.schedule == \"29 8 * * *\":\n        # we don't want to run the mem leak check or disabled tests on normal\n        # periodically scheduled jobs, only the ones at this time\n        filtered_test_matrix = set_periodic_modes(filtered_test_matrix, args.job_name)\n\n    if args.workflow and args.job_name and args.branch not in EXCLUDED_BRANCHES:\n        # If both workflow and job name are available, we will check if the current job\n        # is disabled and remove it and all its dependants from the test matrix\n        filtered_test_matrix = remove_disabled_jobs(\n            args.workflow, args.job_name, filtered_test_matrix\n        )\n\n        filtered_test_matrix = mark_unstable_jobs(\n            args.workflow, args.job_name, filtered_test_matrix\n        )\n\n    pr_body = get_pr_info(int(pr_number)).get(\"body\", \"\") if pr_number else \"\"\n\n    perform_misc_tasks(\n        labels=labels,\n        test_matrix=filtered_test_matrix,\n        job_name=args.job_name,\n        pr_body=pr_body if pr_body else \"\",\n        branch=args.branch,\n        tag=tag,\n    )\n\n    # Set the filtered test matrix as the output\n    set_output(\"test-matrix\", json.dumps(filtered_test_matrix))\n\n    filtered_test_matrix_len = len(filtered_test_matrix.get(\"include\", []))\n    # and also put a flag if the test matrix is empty, so subsequent jobs can\n    # quickly check it without the need to parse the JSON string\n    set_output(\"is-test-matrix-empty\", filtered_test_matrix_len == 0)\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":5,"cache_creation_input_tokens":4061,"cache_read_input_tokens":73195,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":4061},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01YBKeCsVPDWhWgs7PLnCQZ6","session_id":"b6ec2735-e7f2-4093-acb1-67b55f7ecbd4","uuid":"5cb6d086-5316-4dfa-874d-a1736bb9e9ab"}

pytorch/test-infra/.github/actions/teardown-linux@release/2.10\\n523:         if: always() && steps.check_container_runner.outputs.IN_CONTAINER_RUNNER == 'false'\\n524: \\n525:       # NB: We are currently having an intermittent GPU-related issue on G5 runners with\\n526:       # A10G GPU. Once this happens, trying to reset the GPU as done in setup-nvidia does\\n527:       # not seem to help. Here are some symptoms:\\n528:       #   * Calling nvidia-smi timeouts after 60 second\\n529:       #   * Fail to run nvidia-smi with an unable to determine the device handle for GPU\\n530:       #     unknown error\\n531:       #   * Test fails with a missing CUDA GPU error when initializing CUDA in PyTorch\\n532:       #   * Run docker --gpus all fails with error response from daemon\\n533:       #\\n534:       # As both the root cause and recovery path are unclear, let's take the runner out of\\n535:       # service so that it doesn't get any more jobs\\n536:       - name: Check NVIDIA driver installation step\\n537:         if: failure() && steps.install-nvidia-driver.outputs.has-nvidia == 'true' && !contains(matrix.runner, 'b200')\\n538:         shell: bash\\n539:         run: |\\n540:           set +e\\n541:           set -x\\n542: \\n543:           nvidia-smi\\n544:           # NB: Surprisingly, nvidia-smi command returns successfully with return code 0 even in\\n545:           # the case where the driver has already crashed as it still can get the driver version\\n546:           # and some basic information like the bus ID.  However, the rest of the information\\n547:           # would be missing (ERR!), for example:\\n548:           #\\n549:           # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\\n550:           # | NVIDIA-SMI 525.89.02    Driver Version: 525.89.02    CUDA Version: 12.0     |\\n551:           # |-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\\n552:           # | GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A | Volatile Uncorr. ECC |\\n553:           # | Fan  Temp  Perf  Pwr:Usage/Cap|         Memory-Usage | GPU-Util  Compute M. |\\n554:           # |                               |                      |               MIG M. |\\n555:           # |===============================+======================+======================|\\n556:           # |   0  ERR!                Off  | 00000000:00:1E.0 Off |                 ERR! |\\n557:           # |ERR!  ERR! ERR!    ERR! / ERR! |   4184MiB / 23028MiB |    ERR!      Default |\\n558:           # |                               |                      |                 ERR! |\\n559:           # +-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\\n560:           #\\n561:           # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\\n562:           # | Processes:                                                                  |\\n563:           # |  GPU   GI   CI        PID   Type   Process name                  GPU Memory |\\n564:           # |        ID   ID                                                   Usage      |\\n565:           # |=============================================================================|\\n566:           # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\\n567:           #\\n568:           # This should be reported as a failure instead as it will guarantee to fail when\\n569:           # Docker tries to run with --gpus all\\n570:           #\\n571:           # So, the correct check here is to query one of the missing piece of info like\\n572:           # GPU name, so that the command can fail accordingly\\n573:           nvidia-smi --query-gpu=gpu_name --format=csv,noheader --id=0\\n574:           NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS=$?\\n575: \\n576:           # These are acceptable return code from nvidia-smi as copied from setup-nvidia GitHub action\\n577:           if [ \\\"$NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS\\\" -ne 0 ] && [ \\\"$NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS\\\" -ne 14 ]; then\\n578:             echo \\\"NVIDIA driver installation has failed, shutting down the runner...\\\"\\n579:             .github/scripts/stop_runner_service.sh\\n580:           fi\\n581: \\n582:           # For runner with multiple GPUs, we also want to confirm that the number of GPUs are the\\n583:           # power of 2, i.e. 1, 2, 4, or 8. This is to avoid flaky test issue when one GPU fails\\n584:           # https://github.com/pytorch/test-infra/issues/4000\\n585:           GPU_COUNT=$(nvidia-smi --list-gpus | wc -l)\\n586:           NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS=$?\\n587: \\n588:           # These are acceptable return code from nvidia-smi as copied from setup-nvidia GitHub action\\n589:           if [ \\\"$NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS\\\" -ne 0 ] && [ \\\"$NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS\\\" -ne 14 ]; then\\n590:             echo \\\"NVIDIA driver installation has failed, shutting down the runner...\\\"\\n591:             .github/scripts/stop_runner_service.sh\\n592:           fi\\n593: \\n594:           # Check the GPU count to be a power of 2\\n595:           if [ \\\"$GPU_COUNT\\\" -le 8 ] && [ \\\"$GPU_COUNT\\\" -ne 1 ] && [ \\\"$GPU_COUNT\\\" -ne 2 ] && [ \\\"$GPU_COUNT\\\" -ne 4 ] && [ \\\"$GPU_COUNT\\\" -ne 8 ]; then\\n596:             echo \\\"NVIDIA driver detects $GPU_COUNT GPUs. The runner has a broken GPU, shutting it down...\\\"\\n597:             .github/scripts/stop_runner_service.sh\\n598:           fi\\n599: \\n600:       - name: Cleanup docker\\n601:         if: always() && inputs.build-environment == 'linux-s390x-binary-manywheel'\\n602:         shell: bash\\n603:         run: |\\n604:           # on s390x stop the container for clean worker stop\\n605:           docker stop -a || true\\n606:           docker kill -a || true\\n607: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-evals/pytorch--863edc78@863edc787f5fa7950e0f3f86d4952641e1ef9c2c/-/blob/.github/workflows/_linux-test.yml\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01YBKeCsVPDWhWgs7PLnCQZ6","session_id":"b6ec2735-e7f2-4093-acb1-67b55f7ecbd4","uuid":"a1a5e5c3-605f-4cc3-a53f-b736d32321f7"}

However, the rest of the information\n          # would be missing (ERR!), for example:\n          #\n          # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\n          # | NVIDIA-SMI 525.89.02    Driver Version: 525.89.02    CUDA Version: 12.0     |\n          # |-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n          # | GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A | Volatile Uncorr. Off  | 00000000:00:1E.0 Off |                 ERR! |\n          # |ERR!  ERR! ERR!    ERR! / ERR! |   4184MiB / 23028MiB |    ERR!      Default |\n          # |                               |                      |                 ERR! |\n          # +-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+\n          #\n          # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\n          # | Processes:                                                                  |\n          # |  GPU   GI   CI        PID   Type   Process name                  GPU Memory |\n          # |        ID   ID                                                   Usage      |\n          # |=============================================================================|\n          # +-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+\n          #\n          # This should be reported as a failure instead as it will guarantee to fail when\n          # Docker tries to run with --gpus all\n          #\n          # So, the correct check here is to query one of the missing piece of info like\n          # GPU name, so that the command can fail accordingly\n          nvidia-smi --query-gpu=gpu_name --format=csv,noheader --id=0\n          NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS=$?\n\n          # These are acceptable return code from nvidia-smi as copied from setup-nvidia GitHub action\n          if [ \"$NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS\" -ne 0 ] && [ \"$NVIDIA_SMI_STATUS\" -ne 14 ]; then\n            echo \"NVIDIA driver installation has failed, shutting down the runner...\"\n            .github/scripts/stop_runner_service.sh\n          fi\n\n          # For runner with multiple GPUs, we also want to confirm that the number of GPUs are the\n          # power of 2, i.e. 1, 2, 4, or 8. You can do it easily by running the following command:\\n78:           #           python .github/scripts/update_runner_determinator.py\\n79: \\n80:           \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n81:           This runner determinator is used to determine which set of runners to run a\\n82:           GitHub job on. It uses the first comment of a GitHub issue (by default\\n83:           https://github.com/pytorch/test-infra/issues/5132) to define the configuration\\n84:           of which runners should be used to run which job.\\n85: \\n86:           The configuration has two parts, the settings and a list of opted-in users,\\n87:           separated by a line containing \\\"---\\\".  If the line is not present, the\\n88:           settings are considered to be empty with only the second part, the user\\n89:           list, defined.\\n90: \\n91:           The first part is a YAML block that defines the rollout settings. This can be\\n92:           used to define any settings that are needed to determine which runners to use.\\n93:           It's fields are defined by the RolloutSettings class below.\\n94: \\n95:           The second part is a list of users who are explicitly opted in to the LF fleet.\\n96:           The user list is also a comma separated list of additional features or\\n97:           experiments which the user could be opted in to.\\n98: \\n99:           The user list has the following rules:\\n100: \\n101:           - Users are GitHub usernames, which must start with the @ prefix\\n102:           - Each user is also a comma-separated list of features/experiments to enable\\n103:           - A \\\"#\\\" prefix opts the user out of all experiments\\n104: \\n105:           Example config:\\n106:               # A list of experiments that can be opted into.\\n107:               # This defines the behavior they'll induce when opted into.\\n108:               # Expected syntax is:\\n109:               #   [experiment_name]: # Name of the experiment. The PR number is\\n310:               # embedded in the tag name: ciflow/<name>/<pr-number>\\n311: \\n312:               gh = get_gh_client(github_token)\\n313: \\n314:               if username == \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n368:               Dictionary of users with a list of features they have opted into\\n369:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n370: \\n371: \\n372:           def parse_user_opt_in_from_text(user_optin_text: str) -> UserOptins:\\n373:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n374:               Parse the user opt-in text into a key value pair of username and the list of features they have opted into\\n375: \\n376:               Users are GitHub usernames with the @ prefix. Experiment names should only contain alphanumeric characters, '_', and '-'. They cannot contain spaces, and the special characters '_' and '-' cannot be the first or last characters.\\\"\\n413:               )\\n414:               return False\\n415: \\n416: \\n417:           def parse_settings_from_text(settings_text: str) -> Settings:\\n418:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n419:               Parse the experiments from the issue body into a list of ExperimentSettings\\n420:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n421:               try:\\n422:                   if settings_text:\\n423:                       # Escape the backtick as well so that we can have the settings in a code block on the GH issue\\n424:                       # for easy reading\\n425:                       # Note: Using ascii for the backtick so that the cat step in _runner-determinator.yml doesn't choke on\\n426:                       #       the backtick character in shell commands.\\n427:                       backtick = chr(96)  # backtick character\\n428:                       settings_text = settings_text.strip(f\\\"\\\\r\\\\n\\\\t{backtick} \\\")\\n429:                       settings = load_yaml(settings_text)\\n430: \\n431:                       # For now we just load experiments. \\n464:               If it doesn't contain \\\"---\\\" then the settings are empty and the default values are used.\\n465:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n466:               settings_text, _ = extract_settings_user_opt_in_from_text(rollout_state)\\n467:               return parse_settings_from_text(settings_text)\\n468: \\n469: \\n470:           def parse_users(rollout_state: str) -> You can do it easily by running the following command:\\n78:           #           python .github/scripts/update_runner_determinator.py\\n79: \\n80:           \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n81:           This runner determinator is used to determine which set of runners to run a\\n82:           GitHub job on. It uses the first comment of a GitHub issue (by default\\n83:           https://github.com/pytorch/test-infra/issues/5132) to define the configuration\\n84:           of which runners should be used to run which job.\\n85: \\n86:           The configuration has two parts, the settings and a list of opted-in users,\\n87:           separated by a line containing \\\"---\\\".  If the line is not present, the\\n88:           settings are considered to be empty with only the second part, the user\\n89:           list, defined.\\n90: \\n91:           The first part is a YAML block that defines the rollout settings. This can be\\n92:           used to define any settings that are needed to determine which runners to use.\\n93:           It's fields are defined by the RolloutSettings class below.\\n94: \\n95:           The second part is a list of users who are explicitly opted in to the LF fleet.\\n96:           The user list is also a comma separated list of additional features or\\n97:           experiments which the user could be opted in to.\\n98: \\n99:           The user list has the following rules:\\n100: \\n101:           - Users are GitHub usernames, which must start with the @ prefix\\n102:           - Each user is also a comma-separated list of features/experiments to enable\\n103:           - A \\\"#\\\" prefix opts the user out of all experiments\\n104: \\n105:           Example config:\\n106:               # A list of experiments that can be opted into.\\n107:               # This defines the behavior they'll induce when opted into.\\n108:               # Expected syntax is:\\n109:               #   [experiment_name]: # Name of the experiment. Opt-ins:\\n120:               # Users can opt into the LF fleet by adding their GitHub username to this list\\n121:               # and specifying experiments to enable in a comma-separated list.\\n122:               # To always opt out of an experiment, prefix it with a \\\"-\\\".\\n123:               # Experiments should be from the above list.\\n124: \\n125:               @User1,-lf,split_build\\n126:               @User2,lf\\n127:               @User3,split_build\\n128:           \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n129: \\n130:           import json\\n131:           import logging\\n132:           import os\\n133:           import random\\n134:           import re\\n135:           import sys\\n136:           from argparse import ArgumentParser\\n137:           from collections.abc import Iterable\\n138:           from functools import cache\\n139:           from logging import LogRecord\\n140:           from typing import Any, NamedTuple\\n141:           from urllib.request import Request, urlopen\\n142: \\n143:           import yaml\\n144:           from github import Auth, Github\\n145:           from github.Issue import Issue\\n146: \\n147: \\n148:           DEFAULT_LABEL_PREFIX = \\\"\\\"  # use meta runners\\n149:           WORKFLOW_LABEL_LF = \\\"lf.\\\"  # use runners from the linux foundation\\n150:           WORKFLOW_LABEL_LF_CANARY = \\\"lf.c.\\\"  # use canary runners from the linux foundation\\n151: \\n152:           GITHUB_OUTPUT = os.getenv(\\\"GITHUB_OUTPUT\\\", \\\"\\\")\\n153:           GH_OUTPUT_KEY_AMI = \\\"runner-ami\\\"\\n154:           GH_OUTPUT_KEY_LABEL_TYPE = \\\"label-type\\\"\\n155:           OPT_OUT_LABEL = \\\"no-runner-experiments\\\"\\n156: \\n157:           SETTING_EXPERIMENTS = \\\"experiments\\\"\\n158: \\n159:           LF_FLEET_EXPERIMENT = \\\"lf\\\"\\n160:           CANARY_FLEET_SUFFIX = \\\".c\\\"\\n161: \\n162: \\n163:           class Experiment(NamedTuple):\\n164:               rollout_perc: float = (\\n165:                   0  # Percentage of workflows to experiment on when user is not opted-in.\\n166:               )\\n167:               all_branches: bool = (\\n168:                   False  # If True, the experiment is also enabled on the exception branches\\n169:               )\\n170:               default: bool = (\\n171:                   True  # If True, the experiment is enabled by default for all queries\\n172:               )\\n173: \\n174:               # Add more fields as needed\\n175: \\n176: \\n177:           class Settings(NamedTuple):\\n178:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n179:               Settings for the experiments that can be opted into.\\n180:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n181: \\n182:               experiments: dict[str, Experiment] = {}\\n183: \\n184: \\n185:           class ColorFormatter(logging.Formatter):\\n186:               \\\"\\\"\\\"Color codes the log messages based on the log level\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n187: \\n188:               COLORS = {\\n189:                   \\\"WARNING\\\": \\\"\\\\033[33m\\\",  # Yellow\\n190:                   \\\"ERROR\\\": \\\"\\\\033[31m\\\",  # Red\\n191:                   \\\"CRITICAL\\\": \\\"\\\\033[31m\\\",  # Red\\n192:                   \\\"INFO\\\": \\\"\\\\033[0m\\\",  # Reset\\n193:                   \\\"DEBUG\\\": \\\"\\\\033[0m\\\",  # Reset\\n194:               }\\n195: \\n196:               def format(self, record: LogRecord) -> str:\\n197:                   log_color = self.COLORS.get(record.levelname, \\\"\\\\033[0m\\\")  # Default to reset\\n198:                   record.msg = f\\\"{log_color}{record.msg}\\\\033[0m\\\"\\n199:                   return super().format(record)\\n200: \\n201: \\n202:           handler = logging.StreamHandler()\\n203:           handler.setFormatter(ColorFormatter(fmt=\\\"%(levelname)-8s: %(message)s\\\"))\\n204: \\n205:           log = logging.getLogger(os.path.basename(__file__))\\n206:           log.addHandler(handler)\\n207:           log.setLevel(logging.INFO)\\n208: \\n209: \\n210:           def set_github_output(key: str, value: str) -> None:\\n211:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n212:               Defines outputs of the github action that invokes this script\\n213:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n214:               if not GITHUB_OUTPUT:\\n215:                   # See https://github.blog/changelog/2022-10-11-github-actions-deprecating-save-state-and-set-output-commands/ for deprecation notice\\n216:                   log.warning(\\n217:                       \\\"No env var found for GITHUB_OUTPUT, you must be running this code locally. Falling back to the deprecated print method.\\\"\\n218:                   )\\n219:                   print(f\\\"::set-output name={key}::{value}\\\")\\n220:                   return\\n221: \\n222:               with open(GITHUB_OUTPUT, \\\"a\\\") as f:\\n223:                   log.info(f\\\"Setting output: {key}='{value}'\\\")\\n224:                   f.write(f\\\"{key}={value}\\\\n\\\")\\n225: \\n226: \\n227:           def _str_comma_separated_to_set(value: str) -> frozenset[str]:\\n228:               return frozenset(\\n229:                   filter(lambda itm: itm != \\\"\\\", map(str.strip, value.strip(\\\" \\\\n\\\\t\\\").split(\\\",\\\")))\\n230:               )\\n231: \\n232: \\n233:           def parse_args() -> Any:\\n234:               parser = ArgumentParser(\\\"Get dynamic rollout settings\\\")\\n235:               parser.add_argument(\\\"--github-token\\\", type=str, required=True, help=\\\"GitHub token\\\")\\n236:               parser.add_argument(\\n237:                   \\\"--github-issue-repo\\\",\\n238:                   type=str,\\n239:                   required=False,\\n240:                   default=\\\"pytorch/test-infra\\\",\\n241:                   help=\\\"GitHub repo to get the issue\\\",\\n242:               )\\n243:               parser.add_argument(\\n244:                   \\\"--github-repo\\\",\\n245:                   type=str,\\n246:                   required=True,\\n247:                   help=\\\"GitHub repo where CI is running\\\",\\n248:               )\\n249:               parser.add_argument(\\n250:                   \\\"--github-issue\\\", type=int, required=True, help=\\\"GitHub issue number\\\"\\n251:               )\\n252:               parser.add_argument(\\n253:                   \\\"--github-actor\\\", help=\\\"Current GitHub branch or tag\\\"\\n260:               )\\n261:               parser.add_argument(\\n262:                   \\\"--github-ref-type\\\",\\n263:                   type=str,\\n264:                   required=True,\\n265:                   help=\\\"Current GitHub ref type, branch or tag\\\",\\n266:               )\\n267:               parser.add_argument(\\n268: help=\\\"comma separated list of experiments to check, if omitted all experiments marked with default=True are checked\\\",\\n273:               )\\n274:               parser.add_argument(\\n275:            help=(\\n280:                       \\\"comma separated list of experiments to opt-out of. If unset, no opt-outs will occur. \\\"\\n281:                       \\\"If the same experiment is listed both here and in '--eligible-experiments' opt-out will take priority.\\\"\\n282:                   ),\\n283:               )\\n284:               parser.add_argument(\\n285:                   \\\"--pr-number\\\",\\n286:                   type=str,\\n287:                   required=False,\\n288:                   default=\\\"\\\",\\n289:                   help=\\\"the optional PR number where this is run\\\",\\n290:               )\\n291: \\n292:               return parser.parse_args()\\n293: \\n294: \\n295:           def get_gh_client(github_token: str) -> Github:  # type: ignore[no-any-unimported]\\n296:               auth = Auth.Token(github_token)\\n297:               return Github(auth=auth)\\n298: \\n299: \\n300:           def get_issue(gh: Github, repo: str, issue_num: int) -> Issue:  # type: ignore[no-any-unimported]\\n301:               repo = gh.get_repo(repo)\\n302:               return repo.get_issue(number=issue_num)\\n303: \\n304: \\n305:           def get_potential_pr_author(\\n306:               github_token: str, repo: str, username: str, ref_type: str, ref_name: str\\n307:           ) -> str:\\n308:               # If the trigger was a new tag added by a bot, this is a ciflow case\\n309:               # Fetch the actual username from the original PR. The PR number is\\n310:               # embedded in the tag name: ciflow/<name>/<pr-number>\\n311: \\n312:               gh = get_gh_client(github_token)\\n313: \\n314:               if username == \\\"pytorch-bot[bot]\\\" and ref_type == \\\"tag\\\":\\n315:                   split_tag = ref_name.split(\\\"/\\\")\\n316:                   if (\\n317:                       len(split_tag) == 3\\n318:                       and split_tag[0] == \\\"ciflow\\\"\\n319:                       and split_tag[2].isnumeric()\\n320:                   ):\\n321:                       pr_number = split_tag[2]\\n322:                       try:\\n323:                           repository = gh.get_repo(repo)\\n324:                           pull = repository.get_pull(number=int(pr_number))\\n325:                       except Exception as e:\\n326:                           raise Exception(  # noqa: TRY002\\n327:                               f\\\"issue with pull request {pr_number} from repo {repository}\\\"\\n328:                           ) from e\\n329:                       return pull.user.login  # type: ignore[no-any-return]\\n330:               # In all other cases, return the original input username\\n331:               return username\\n332: \\n333: \\n334:           def is_exception_branch(branch: str) -> bool:\\n335:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n336:               Branches that get opted out of experiments by default, until they're explicitly enabled.\\n337:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n338:               return branch.split(\\\"/\\\")[0] in {\\\"main\\\", \\\"nightly\\\", \\\"release\\\", \\\"landchecks\\\"}\\n339: \\n340: \\n341:           def load_yaml(yaml_text: str) -> Any:\\n342:               try:\\n343:                   data = yaml.safe_load(yaml_text)\\n344:                   return data\\n345:               except yaml.YAMLError:\\n346:                   log.exception(\\\"Error loading YAML\\\")\\n347:                   raise\\n348: \\n349: \\n350:           def extract_settings_user_opt_in_from_text(rollout_state: str) -> tuple[str, str]:\\n351:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n352:               Extracts the text with settings, if any, and the opted in users from the rollout state.\\n353: \\n354:               If the issue body contains \\\"---\\\" then the text above that is the settings\\n355:               and the text below is the list of opted in users.\\n356: \\n357:               If it doesn't contain \\\"---\\\" then the settings are empty and the rest is the users.\\n358:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n359:               rollout_state_parts = rollout_state.split(\\\"---\\\")\\n360:               if len(rollout_state_parts) >= 2:\\n361:                   return rollout_state_parts[0], rollout_state_parts[1]\\n362:               else:\\n363:                   return \\\"\\\", rollout_state\\n364: \\n365: \\n366:           class UserOptins(dict[str, list[str]]):\\n367:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n368:               Dictionary of users with a list of features they have opted into\\n369:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n370: \\n371: \\n372:           def parse_user_opt_in_from_text(user_optin_text: str) -> UserOptins:\\n373:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n374:               Parse the user opt-in text into a key value pair of username and the list of features they have opted into\\n375: \\n376:               Users are GitHub usernames with the @ prefix. Each user is also a comma-separated list of features/experiments to enable.\\n377:                   - Example line: \\\"@User1,lf,split_build\\\"\\n378:                   - A \\\"#\\\" prefix indicates the user is opted out of all experiments\\n379: \\n380: \\n381:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n382:               optins = UserOptins()\\n383:               for user in user_optin_text.split(\\\"\\\\n\\\"):\\n384:                   user = user.strip(\\\"\\\\r\\\\n\\\\t -\\\")\\n385:                   if not user or not user.startswith(\\\"@\\\"):\\n386:                       # Not a valid user. Skip\\n387:                       continue\\n388: \\n389:                   if user:\\n390:                       usr_name = user.split(\\\",\\\")[0].strip(\\\"@\\\")\\n391:                       optins[usr_name] = [exp.strip(\\\" \\\") for exp in user.split(\\\",\\\")[1:]]\\n392: \\n393:               return optins\\n394: \\n395: \\n396:           def is_valid_experiment_name(experiment_name: str) -> bool:\\n397:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n398:               Check if the experiment name is valid.\\n399:               A valid name:\\n400:                   - Contains only alphanumeric characters and the special characters \\\"_\\\" & \\\"-\\\"\\n401:                   - The special characters \\\"_\\\" & \\\"-\\\" shouldn't be the first or last characters\\n402:                   - Cannot contain spaces\\n403:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n404: \\n405:               valid_char_regex = r\\\"^[a-zA-Z0-9]([\\\\w-]*[a-zA-Z0-9])?$\\\"\\n406:               valid = bool(re.match(valid_char_regex, experiment_name))\\n407: \\n408:               if valid:\\n409:                   return True\\n410: \\n411:               log.error(\\n412:                   f\\\"Invalid experiment name: {experiment_name}. Experiment names should only contain alphanumeric characters, '_', and '-'. They cannot contain spaces, and the special characters '_' and '-' cannot be the first or last characters.\\\"\\n413:               )\\n414:               return False\\n415: \\n416: \\n417:           def parse_settings_from_text(settings_text: str) -> Settings:\\n418:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n419:               Parse the experiments from the issue body into a list of ExperimentSettings\\n420:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n421:               try:\\n422:                   if settings_text:\\n423:                       # Escape the backtick as well so that we can have the settings in a code block on the GH issue\\n424:                       # for easy reading\\n425:                       # Note: Using ascii for the backtick so that the cat step in _runner-determinator.yml doesn't choke on\\n426:                       #       the backtick character in shell commands.\\n427:                       backtick = chr(96)  # backtick character\\n428:                       settings_text = settings_text.strip(f\\\"\\\\r\\\\n\\\\t{backtick} \\\")\\n429:                       settings = load_yaml(settings_text)\\n430: \\n431:                       # For now we just load experiments. We can expand this if/when we add more settings\\n432:                       experiments = {}\\n433: \\n434:                       for exp_name, exp_settings in settings.get(SETTING_EXPERIMENTS).items():\\n435:                           if not is_valid_experiment_name(exp_name):\\n436:                               # Exclude invalid experiments from the list. We log an error, but don't raise an exception so that other experiments can still be processed.\\n437:                               continue\\n438: \\n439:                           valid_settings = {}\\n440:                           for setting in exp_settings:\\n441:                               if setting not in Experiment._fields:\\n442:                                   log.warning(\\n443:                                       f\\\"Unexpected setting in experiment: {setting} = {exp_settings[setting]}\\\"\\n444:                                   )\\n445:                               else:\\n446:                                   valid_settings[setting] = exp_settings[setting]\\n447: \\n448:                           experiments[exp_name] = Experiment(**valid_settings)\\n449:                       return Settings(experiments)\\n450: \\n451:               except Exception:\\n452:                   log.exception(\\\"Failed to parse settings\\\")\\n453: \\n454:               return Settings()\\n455: \\n456: \\n457:           def parse_settings(rollout_state: str) -> Settings:\\n458:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n459:               Parse settings, if any, from the rollout state.\\n460: \\n461:               If the issue body contains \\\"---\\\" then the text above that is the settings\\n462:               and the text below is the list of opted in users.\\n463: \\n464:               If it doesn't contain \\\"---\\\" then the settings are empty and the default values are used.\\n465:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n466:               settings_text, _ = extract_settings_user_opt_in_from_text(rollout_state)\\n467:               return parse_settings_from_text(settings_text)\\n468: \\n469: \\n470:           def parse_users(rollout_state: str) -> UserOptins:\\n471:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n472:               Parse users from the rollout state.\\n473: \\n474:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n475:               _, users_text = extract_settings_user_opt_in_from_text(rollout_state)\\n476:               return parse_user_opt_in_from_text(users_text)\\n477: \\n478: \\n479:           def is_user_opted_in(user: str, user_optins: UserOptins, experiment_name: str) -> bool:\\n480:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n481:               Check if a user is opted into an experiment\\n482:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n483:               return experiment_name in user_optins.get(user, [])\\n484: \\n485: \\n486:           def is_user_opted_out(user: str, user_optins: UserOptins, experiment_name: str) -> bool:\\n487:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n488:               Check if a user explicitly opted out of an experiment\\n489:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n490:               # if the experiment is prefixed with a \\\"-\\\", then it's an opt-out\\n491:               experiment_optout = \\\"-\\\" + experiment_name\\n492:               if experiment_optout not in user_optins.get(user, []):\\n493:                   return False\\n494: \\n495:               if is_user_opted_in(user, user_optins, experiment_name):\\n496:                   log.warning(\\n497:                       f\\\"User {user} is opted into experiment {experiment_name}, but also opted out of it. Defaulting to opting out\\\"\\n498:                   )\\n499: \\n500:               return True\\n501: \\n502: \\n503:           def get_runner_prefix(\\n504:               rollout_state: str,\\n505:               workflow_requestors: Iterable[str],\\n506:               branch: str,\\n507:               eligible_experiments: frozenset[str] = frozenset(),\\n508:               opt_out_experiments: frozenset[str] = frozenset(),\\n509:               is_canary: bool = False,\\n510:           ) -> str:\\n511:               settings = parse_settings(rollout_state)\\n512:               user_optins = parse_users(rollout_state)\\n513: \\n514:               fleet_prefix = \\\"\\\"\\n515:               prefixes = []\\n516:               for experiment_name, experiment_settings in settings.experiments.items():\\n517:                   if not experiment_settings.all_branches and is_exception_branch(branch):\\n518:                       log.info(\\n519:                           f\\\"Branch {branch} is an exception branch. Not enabling experiment {experiment_name}.\\\"\\n520:                       )\\n521:                       continue\\n522: \\n523:                   if opt_out_experiments:\\n524:                       if experiment_name in opt_out_experiments:\\n525:                           opt_out_exp_list = \\\", \\\".join(opt_out_experiments)\\n526:                           log.info(\\n527:                               f\\\"Skipping experiment '{experiment_name}', as this workflow has opted-out (opted out experiments are: {opt_out_exp_list})\\\"\\n528:                           )\\n529:                           continue\\n530: \\n531:                   if eligible_experiments:\\n532:                       if experiment_name not in eligible_experiments:\\n533:                           exp_list = \\\", \\\".join(eligible_experiments)\\n534:                           log.info(\\n535:                               f\\\"Skipping experiment '{experiment_name}', as it is not in the eligible_experiments list: {exp_list}\\\"\\n536:                           )\\n537:                           continue\\n538:                   elif not experiment_settings.default:\\n539:                       log.info(\\n540:                           f\\\"Skipping experiment '{experiment_name}', as it is not a default experiment\\\"\\n541:                       )\\n542:                       continue\\n543: \\n544:                   # Is any workflow_requestor opted out to this experiment?\\n545:                   opted_out_users = [\\n546:                       requestor\\n547:                       for requestor in workflow_requestors\\n548:                       if is_user_opted_out(requestor, user_optins, experiment_name)\\n549:                   ]\\n550: \\n551:                   if opted_out_users:\\n552:                       log.info(\\n553:                           f\\\"{', '.join(opted_out_users)} have opted out of experiment {experiment_name}.\\\"\\n554:                       )\\n555:                       continue\\n556: \\n557:                   # Is any workflow_requestor opted in to this experiment?\\n558:                   opted_in_users = [\\n559:                       requestor\\n560:                       for requestor in workflow_requestors\\n561:                       if is_user_opted_in(requestor, user_optins, experiment_name)\\n562:                   ]\\n563: \\n564:                   enabled = False\\n565:                   if opted_in_users:\\n566:                       log.info(\\n567:                           f\\\"{', '.join(opted_in_users)} have opted into experiment {experiment_name}.\\\"\\n568:                       )\\n569:                       enabled = True\\n570: \\n571:                   elif experiment_settings.rollout_perc:\\n572:                       # If no user is opted in, then we randomly enable the experiment based on the rollout percentage\\n573:                       if random.uniform(0, 100) <= experiment_settings.rollout_perc:\\n574:                           log.info(\\n575:                               f\\\"Based on rollout percentage of {experiment_settings.rollout_perc}%, enabling experiment {experiment_name}.\\\"\\n576:                           )\\n577:                           enabled = True\\n578: \\n579:                   if enabled:\\n580:                       label = experiment_name\\n581:                       if experiment_name == LF_FLEET_EXPERIMENT:\\n582:                           # We give some special treatment to the \\\"lf\\\" experiment since determines the fleet we use\\n583:                           #  - If it's enabled, then we always list it's prefix first\\n584:                           #  - If we're in the canary branch, then we append \\\".c\\\" to the lf prefix\\n585:                           if is_canary:\\n586:                               label += CANARY_FLEET_SUFFIX\\n587:                           fleet_prefix = label\\n588:                       else:\\n589:                           prefixes.append(label)\\n590: \\n591:               if len(prefixes) > 1:\\n592:                   log.error(\\n593:                       f\\\"Only a fleet and one other experiment can be enabled for a job at any time. Enabling {prefixes[0]} and ignoring the rest, which are {', '.join(prefixes[1:])}\\\"\\n594:                   )\\n595:                   prefixes = prefixes[:1]\\n596: \\n597:               # Fleet always comes first\\n598:               if fleet_prefix:\\n599:                   prefixes.insert(0, fleet_prefix)\\n600: \\n601:               return \\\".\\\".join(prefixes) + \\\".\\\" if prefixes else \\\"\\\"\\n602: \\n603: \\n604:           def get_rollout_state_from_issue(github_token: str, repo: str, issue_num: int) -> str:\\n605:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n606:               Gets the first comment of the issue, which contains the desired rollout state.\\n607: \\n608:               The default issue we use - https://github.com/pytorch/test-infra/issues/5132\\n609:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n610:               gh = get_gh_client(github_token)\\n611:               issue = get_issue(gh, repo, issue_num)\\n612:               return str(issue.get_comments()[0].body.strip(\\\"\\\\n\\\\t \\\"))\\n613: \\n614: \\n615:           def download_json(url: str, headers: dict[str, str], num_retries: int = 3) -> Any:\\n616:               for _ in range(num_retries):\\n617:                   try:\\n618:                       req = Request(url=url, headers=headers)\\n619:                       content = urlopen(req, timeout=5).read().decode(\\\"utf-8\\\")\\n620:                       return json.loads(content)\\n621:                   except Exception as e:\\n622:                       log.warning(f\\\"Could not download {url}: {e}\\\")\\n623: \\n624:               log.warning(f\\\"All {num_retries} retries exhausted, downloading {url} failed\\\")\\n625:               return {}\\n626: \\n627: \\n628:           @cache\\n629:           def get_pr_info(github_repo: str, github_token: str, pr_number: int) -> dict[str, Any]:\\n630:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n631:               Dynamically get PR information\\n632:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n633:               github_api = f\\\"https://api.github.com/repos/{github_repo}\\\"\\n634:               headers = {\\n635:                   \\\"Accept\\\": \\\"application/vnd.github.v3+json\\\",\\n636:                   \\\"Authorization\\\": f\\\"token {github_token}\\\",\\n637:               }\\n638:               json_response: dict[str, Any] = download_json(\\n639:                   url=f\\\"{github_api}/issues/{pr_number}\\\",\\n640:                   headers=headers,\\n641:               )\\n642: \\n643:               if not json_response:\\n644:                   log.warning(f\\\"Failed to get the labels for #{pr_number}\\\")\\n645:                   return {}\\n646: \\n647:               return json_response\\n648: \\n649: \\n650:           def get_labels(github_repo: str, github_token: str, pr_number: int) -> set[str]:\\n651:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n652:               Dynamically get the latest list of labels from the pull request\\n653:               \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n654:               pr_info = get_pr_info(github_repo, github_token, pr_number)\\n655:               return {\\n656:                   label.get(\\\"name\\\") for label in pr_info.get(\\\"labels\\\", []) if label.get(\\\"name\\\")\\n657:               }\\n658: \\n659: \\n660:           def main() -> None:\\n661:               args = parse_args()\\n662: \\n663:               runner_label_prefix = DEFAULT_LABEL_PREFIX\\n664: \\n665:               # Check if the PR is opt-out\\n666:               if args.pr_number:\\n667:                   labels = get_labels(args.github_repo, args.github_token, int(args.pr_number))\\n668:                   if OPT_OUT_LABEL in labels:\\n669:                       log.info(\\n670:                           f\\\"Opt-out runner determinator because #{args.pr_number} has {OPT_OUT_LABEL} label\\\"\\n671:                       )\\n672:                       set_github_output(GH_OUTPUT_KEY_LABEL_TYPE, runner_label_prefix)\\n673:                       sys.exit()\\n674: \\n675:               try:\\n676:                   rollout_state = get_rollout_state_from_issue(\\n677:                       args.github_token, args.github_issue_repo, args.github_issue\\n678:                   )\\n679: \\n680:                   username = get_potential_pr_author(\\n681:                       args.github_token,\\n682:                       args.github_repo,\\n683:                       args.github_actor,\\n684:
